food_name: Baguette with Sunny-Side-Up Egg (Banh Mi Op La)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Banh Mi Op La; 'sandwich food product' is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific Vietnamese baguette sandwich class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Sandwiches) is the closest defensible category. No specific Banh Mi Op La code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Banh Mi Op La or Vietnamese baguette sandwich as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one baguette sandwich with a sunny-side-up egg, weighing approximately 350 g, as typically sold at Vietnamese breakfast stalls and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg sandwich on baguette-type roll (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for French baguette (FDC ID 172686) and fried egg (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Banh Mi Op La. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for French baguette (~60% by weight) and pan-fried egg (~25% by weight), with the remaining ~15% attributed to fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, cucumber, fish sauce, and soy sauce. The input record values (energy 128.57 kcal, protein 5.14 g, fat 5.71 g, carbohydrate 14.29 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 257 mg/100 g reflects the fish sauce and soy sauce contribution and is plausible. All values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172686 (French bread) and FDC ID 173424 (Egg, fried).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread / French baguette
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banh Mi Op La. GI of 75 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white French baguette (GI ~75, glucose reference), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source (~60% by weight). The egg and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 × 50.0 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 37.5. Available carbohydrate per serving: 14.29 g/100 g × 350 g = 50.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 257 mg/100 g (900 mg per standard 350 g serving) from fish sauce, soy sauce, and baguette; potassium is low and dietary fibre is only 0.86 g/100 g, providing insufficient offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving, request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, substitute low-sodium soy sauce, and increase the proportion of cucumber and pickled vegetables to improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white baguette (GI ~75) with 14.29 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 0.86 g dietary fibre/100 g causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; glycemic load per standard serving is 37.5, which is clinically high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a wholegrain or multigrain roll to lower the GI, retain the egg for its protein-mediated glucose-moderating effect, avoid added sugar in the dipping sauce, and limit portion to half a serving to reduce glycemic load to an acceptable range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 257 mg/100 g (900 mg per serving) is elevated; cholesterol is 57.14 mg/100 g (200 mg per serving) from the fried egg; saturated fat is 1.43 g/100 g; trans fat is present at 0.03 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, reduce fish sauce and soy sauce to lower sodium, use a cooking spray instead of butter or oil for the egg to reduce saturated fat, and pair with fresh vegetables to increase dietary fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No meaningful anti-inflammatory factors present; refined baguette drives high glycaemic load; chilli is a nightshade ingredient; no omega-3, polyphenols, or cruciferous vegetables.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should omit chilli (nightshade), request wholegrain bread, add anti-inflammatory toppings such as avocado or fresh ginger, and reduce fish sauce and soy sauce to lower sodium and inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Mì Ốp La (Baguette with Sunny-Side-Up Egg) is a classic Vietnamese breakfast dish consisting of a crispy French baguette served open or closed with a pan-fried sunny-side-up egg, fresh herbs such as coriander and spring onion, pickled daikon and carrot, sliced cucumber, and a savoury dipping sauce made from fish sauce and soy sauce, often with chilli. The baguette is a legacy of French colonial influence in Vietnam and is characteristically light and crispy with a thin crust. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a unit: the baguette is baked separately, the egg is pan-fried in oil until the white is set and the yolk remains runny, and the accompaniments are arranged alongside or inside the bread. The flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, and slightly tangy from the pickled vegetables, with a contrast of textures between the crispy bread, soft egg yolk, and crunchy cucumber. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 450 kcal, 18 g protein, 20 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~900 mg per serving) due to fish sauce and soy sauce, and has a high glycemic index (~75) driven by the white baguette. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification. The egg contributes cholesterol (~200 mg per serving) and some saturated fat. The dish is not vegetarian due to fish sauce. It is commonly consumed as breakfast or a light meal in Vietnam and at Vietnamese food stalls in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Banh Mi Op La. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for French baguette and fried egg, which are the dominant components. The input record values from the GrabFood-sourced AI-generated record are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white French baguette as the analogous food. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese baguette-and-egg dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.03 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with trace levels in pan-fried egg and baguette; this is a plausible trace value retained from the source record. All core macros (protein_g 5.14, fat_g 5.71, carbohydrate_g 14.29) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a baguette-and-egg composite dish. No zero-macro justification is required. The dish is not halal-certified as fish sauce of uncertain halal status is a standard ingredient. Chilli is a nightshade ingredient relevant to rheumatoid arthritis caution. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white French baguette from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Banh Mi Op La exists in the approved literature as of 2024.
